completeness_note: Expanded 2026-07-16 with high-confusion exam terms across governance, reserves, EEF/OPA, risk/issue vocabulary, Scrum, flow metrics, and organizational structures. Not an exhaustive PMP dictionary; additions go through the same review.

entries.definition: A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique result and enable intended value.
entries.common_confusion: A recurring operational process can produce deliverables but is not temporary or unique in the same way.
entries.exam_trap: Treating any organized work as a project without checking temporary and unique characteristics.

entries.definition: A coordinated group of related projects and activities managed together to obtain benefits unavailable from separate management.
entries.common_confusion: A large project is not automatically a program.
entries.exam_trap: Choosing program governance solely because work is complex or long.

entries.definition: Projects, programs, and other work grouped to support strategic objectives and investment decisions.
entries.common_confusion: Portfolio components need not be operationally related.
entries.exam_trap: Assuming portfolio management coordinates shared delivery dependencies like program management.

entries.definition: Ongoing, repeatable work that sustains the organization, products, or services.
entries.common_confusion: Operational improvement may be delivered through a project, but the resulting ongoing process remains operations.
entries.exam_trap: Keeping a project open indefinitely because its output continues to operate.

entries.definition: An organizational structure that standardizes, supports, controls, or directs project-management practices according to its mandate.
entries.common_confusion: PMO authority varies; not every PMO directs project managers.
entries.exam_trap: Escalating every decision to the PMO without checking governance and authority.

entries.definition: The justification for an initiative based on expected value, feasibility, costs, benefits, risks, and alternatives.
entries.common_confusion: The charter authorizes a project; the business case explains why it should be pursued.
entries.exam_trap: Using the business case as the detailed execution plan.

entries.definition: A hierarchical decomposition of the total project scope into manageable components.
entries.common_confusion: A WBS organizes scope, not calendar dates or the organization chart.
entries.exam_trap: Treating an activity list or schedule as the scope baseline.

entries.definition: The longest-duration path through a schedule network that determines the earliest project completion under the current model.
entries.common_confusion: Critical does not mean most important or most expensive.
entries.exam_trap: Assuming the critical path never changes after schedule updates.

entries.definition: The budgeted value of work actually completed at a point in time.
entries.common_confusion: EV is not the amount spent and is not the planned value of scheduled work.
entries.exam_trap: Comparing EV to budget at completion without considering the question being asked.

entries.definition: The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to pursue or retain in support of objectives.
entries.common_confusion: Appetite is broader than a specific measurable risk threshold.
entries.exam_trap: Applying the project manager's personal tolerance instead of organizational guidance.

entries.definition: A Scrum accountability focused on establishing Scrum, improving team effectiveness, coaching, and removing organizational impediments.
entries.common_confusion: The Scrum Master is not the team's task-assigning project manager.
entries.exam_trap: Making the Scrum Master the owner of product priority or individual work assignments.

entries.definition: An ordered, evolving collection of work needed to improve a product and pursue product goals.
entries.common_confusion: A backlog is not a frozen predictive scope baseline.
entries.exam_trap: Treating every requested item as committed work regardless of value, readiness, or priority.

entries.definition: Work that has started but has not yet reached the defined completion point in a flow system.
entries.common_confusion: Starting more work can reduce flow rather than increase throughput.
entries.exam_trap: Optimizing individual utilization while ignoring queue growth and cycle time.

entries.definition: The governed use of AI with human accountability and controls for privacy, bias, transparency, safety, security, copyright, and impact.
entries.common_confusion: Human review alone is not sufficient if data, authority, or audit controls are absent.
entries.exam_trap: Delegating final accountability to an AI system because its recommendation appears confident.

entries.definition: The document that formally authorizes the project and gives the project manager authority to apply organizational resources.
entries.common_confusion: The business case justifies the investment; the charter authorizes the project.
entries.exam_trap: Assuming a project manager can commit resources before charter approval.

entries.definition: The approved version of a plan component (scope, schedule, cost) used as the reference for measuring performance.
entries.common_confusion: A plan describes intent; the baseline is the frozen approved reference that variances are measured against.
entries.exam_trap: Updating a baseline informally to match actual progress instead of through change control.

entries.definition: The governance body that reviews, approves, or rejects change requests above delegated thresholds.
entries.common_confusion: The board decides; the project manager analyzes impact and recommends.
entries.exam_trap: The project manager approving above-threshold changes personally to save time.

entries.definition: A formal proposal to modify a document, deliverable, or baseline, evaluated through integrated change control.
entries.common_confusion: Corrective action, preventive action, and defect repair are all raised as change requests.
entries.exam_trap: Implementing verbally requested "small tweaks" without a formal change request.

entries.definition: Budget or time set aside for identified risks, sized by risk analysis and held within the baseline.
entries.common_confusion: Management reserve covers unidentified risks and sits above the baseline.
entries.exam_trap: Drawing management reserve for a risk that was identified and planned for.

entries.definition: Budget held outside the cost baseline for unforeseen work within project scope — unknown unknowns.
entries.common_confusion: Not the project manager’s to spend at discretion; release requires management approval.
entries.exam_trap: Funding new scope from management reserve instead of change control and budgeting.

entries.definition: Internal or external conditions, not controlled by the team, that influence or constrain the project — culture, regulations, market conditions, infrastructure.
entries.common_confusion: OPAs are the organization’s own assets; EEFs are conditions imposed on the work.
entries.exam_trap: Classifying a government regulation as an OPA because the organization must implement it.

entries.definition: The organization’s accumulated assets available to projects — templates, procedures, historical data, lessons learned repositories.
entries.common_confusion: EEFs constrain from outside the team’s control; OPAs are usable organizational property.
entries.exam_trap: Treating a company template pulled from the process library as an EEF.

entries.definition: A present, certain problem currently affecting the project, tracked in the issue log with an owner and target resolution date.
entries.common_confusion: A risk is uncertain and future; an issue is certain and present.
entries.exam_trap: Leaving a materialized risk in the risk register instead of transitioning it to issue management.

entries.definition: A factor treated as true for planning purposes without proof, recorded in the assumption log because it can fail.
entries.common_confusion: Constraints are known, given limits; assumptions are unproven planning premises.
entries.exam_trap: Defending the plan when an assumption fails instead of reassessing through change control.

entries.definition: A known limiting factor the project must operate within — a fixed date, capped budget, or mandated technology.
entries.common_confusion: An assumption might prove false; a constraint is a given boundary.
entries.exam_trap: Accepting a constraint change as free instead of analyzing impact across the other constraints.

entries.definition: The project document recording identified stakeholders, their interests, influence, and engagement information.
entries.common_confusion: A living document — new phases, markets, and reorganizations add stakeholders after initiation.
entries.exam_trap: Treating the initiation-time register as final while the stakeholder landscape shifts.

entries.definition: A fixed-length timebox (one month or less) in which a Scrum team produces a done, usable increment.
entries.common_confusion: The sprint’s commitment is protected; change enters through the backlog at planning boundaries.
entries.exam_trap: Absorbing mid-sprint scope injections instead of routing them to the backlog.

entries.definition: A concrete, usable addition of working product that meets the definition of done.
entries.common_confusion: Work that misses the definition of done is inventory, not an increment.
entries.exam_trap: Counting demo-ready but not-done work as a completed increment.

entries.definition: The shared, explicit quality standard an increment must meet to count as complete.
entries.common_confusion: Acceptance criteria are per-item; the definition of done applies to every increment.
entries.exam_trap: Letting the product owner waive the definition of done item-by-item.

entries.definition: The single person accountable for maximizing product value and ordering the product backlog.
entries.common_confusion: Stakeholders and sponsors influence; the Product Owner decides backlog order.
entries.exam_trap: Letting a sponsor or the Scrum Master reprioritize the backlog directly.

entries.definition: A team’s demonstrated average of story points completed per iteration, used as an empirical planning input.
entries.common_confusion: Team-relative — velocities are not comparable across teams.
entries.exam_trap: Using velocity as a performance target, which invites point inflation.

entries.definition: Elapsed time from request to delivery — the customer’s clock, including queue wait.
entries.common_confusion: Cycle time starts when work starts; lead time starts when the request arrives.
entries.exam_trap: Explaining a long lead time by pointing only at active work time.

entries.definition: Elapsed time from work started to work finished — the team’s clock on an item.
entries.common_confusion: Little’s Law ties it to WIP and throughput: cycle time = WIP ÷ throughput.
entries.exam_trap: Trying to shorten cycle time by starting more work in parallel — WIP growth lengthens it.

entries.definition: The smallest thing that tests the riskiest assumption with real users; its output is validated learning.
entries.common_confusion: An MMF delivers standalone marketable value; an MVP buys learning.
entries.exam_trap: Reading ‘minimum’ as permission for low quality rather than small scope.

entries.definition: A responsibility assignment matrix marking who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed for each work item.
entries.common_confusion: Exactly one Accountable per item; several people can be Responsible.
entries.exam_trap: Assigning two Accountable parties and calling the ambiguity collaboration.

entries.definition: A structure where staff report to both functional and project managers, with authority split by matrix strength (weak, balanced, strong).
entries.common_confusion: In a weak matrix the functional manager holds most authority; in a strong matrix the project manager does.
entries.exam_trap: Prescribing directive PM behavior in a weak matrix where negotiation is the real tool.

entries.definition: A review at a phase end deciding continue, modify, repeat, or stop, based on current evidence.
entries.common_confusion: Also called stage gate or kill point; it exists to stop bad investments continuing on momentum.
entries.exam_trap: Letting sunk cost reasoning drive a continue decision at the gate.

entries.definition: The project document capturing knowledge gained, feeding the organizational repository as a process asset.
entries.common_confusion: Captured throughout the project, not only at closure.
entries.exam_trap: Deferring lessons capture until after the team has dispersed.
